coredata.dc:title: Microwave absorption study of composites based on CQD@BaTiO3 core shell and BaFe12O19 nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Goel, Shivanshu
Tyagi, Ankit
Garg, Avesh
Kumar, Sumit
Baskey, Himangshu Bhusan
Gupta, Raju Kumar
Tyagi, Sachin
coredata.dc:description: A novel microwave absorbing composite medium for X-band (8 GHz–12GHz) is developed by mixing of core-shell consisting of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) coated barium titanate (BaTiO3) fibers with barium hexaferrite (BaFe12O19) nanoparticles. CQDs are synthesized from agricultural waste using lemon peel as precursor material while the low density BaTiO3 fibers are synthesized via electro-spinning technique. The increase in diameter of BaTiO3 fibers as an effect of CQD coating has been confirmed using FESEM technique. The XRD and FTIR analysis of CQD coated BaTiO3 fibers are used to confirm the presence of carbon along with barium titanate. Composites based on BaFe12O19 nanoparticles and CQD coated barium titanate (CQD@BaTiO3) fibers were developed by the ball-milling of BaFe12O19 nanoparticles with CQD@BaTiO3 in different weight concentrations. The improved electrical permittivity of the BaFe12O19 and CQD@BaTiO3 composites resulted in the increased reflection loss due to better impedance matching with the free space. The maximum reflection loss of −25.38 dB was achieved for composite containing 85% of BaFe12O19 and 15% of CQD@BaTiO3, for a pellet of thickness 2.75 mm. Also, the maximum −10dB bandwidth (90% absorption) of the composite reaches to 2.7 GHz.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Composite
Core-shell
Reflection loss
Microwave absorption
Permittivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots
Synthesis of core-shell heterostructure of CQD@ BaTiO3 fibers
Complex permittivity and complex permeability study

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to Director, Directorate of Extramural Research & Intellectual Property Rights, DRDO Delhi for providing financial support to carry out research. The authors would also like to acknowledge the Director, DRDO-DMSRDE, Kanpur for providing support in carrying out the reflection loss study. The authors are thankful to the Director, CSIR-CSIO , and IIT- Kanpur for the support and the encouragement provided for the present work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic for the synthesis of carbon quantum dot coated barium titanate fibers.
Schematic for synthesis of BaFe12O19 nanoparticles.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra (b) TEM micrographs (inset: size distribution histogram) of Carbon quantum dots.
FESEM images of (a) BaTiO3 fibers, (b) CQD@BaTiO3 fibers and (c) BaFe12O19 nanoparticles; (d) FESEM and (e) EDAX of BCBT(15) composite.
Indexed XRD patterns of (a) BaTiO3 fibers and CQD@BaTiO3 fibers, (b) BaFe12O19 nanoparticles and (c) BCBT(15) composite.
FTIR spectrum of (a) BaTiO3 fibers and CQD@BaTiO3 fibers (b) BaFe12O19 nanoparticles.
Showing the variation of (a) real part, (b) imaginary part of permittivity of BaFe12O19/CQD@ BaTiO3 composites.
Showing the variation of (a) real part, (b) imaginary part of permeability of BaFe12O19/CQD@ BaTiO3 composites.
(a)Dielectric loss tangent and (b) Magnetic loss tangent curves of BaFe12O19/CQD@ BaTiO3 composites.
3-D representation of reflection loss curves of a) BCBT(0), b) BCBT(5), c) BCBT(10) and d) BCBT(15) composites.
a) Reflection loss curves and b) Normalized impedance plot of BaFe12O19/CQD@ BaTiO3 composites at thickness 2.75 mm.
Details of developed composites of BaFe12O19/CQD@BaTiO3.
Reflection Loss results of developed composites at 2.75 mm.

head.ce:title: Microwave absorption study of composites based on CQD@BaTiO3 core shell and BaFe12O19 nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . CSIR-Central Scientific Instruments Organization, Chandigarh-160030, India

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research, Ghaziabad-201002, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Special Materials Group, DRDO-DMSRDE, Kanpur, 208013, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Special Materials Group, DRDO-DMSRDE, Kanpur-208013, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Ubiquitous Analytical Techniques, CSIR-Central Scientific Instruments Organization, Chandigarh 160030, India.
Corresponding author. Department of Chemical Engineering, IIT Kanpur, Kanpur, Uttar Pradesh 208016, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel microwave absorbing composite medium for X-band (8 GHz–12GHz) is developed by mixing of core-shell consisting of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) coated barium titanate (BaTiO3) fibers with barium hexaferrite (BaFe12O19) nanoparticles. CQDs are synthesized from agricultural waste using lemon peel as precursor material while the low density BaTiO3 fibers are synthesized via electro-spinning technique. The increase in diameter of BaTiO3 fibers as an effect of CQD coating has been confirmed using FESEM technique. The XRD and FTIR analysis of CQD coated BaTiO3 fibers are used to confirm the presence of carbon along with barium titanate. Composites based on BaFe12O19 nanoparticles and CQD coated barium titanate (CQD@BaTiO3) fibers were developed by the ball-milling of BaFe12O19 nanoparticles with CQD@BaTiO3 in different weight concentrations. The improved electrical permittivity of the BaFe12O19 and CQD@BaTiO3 composites resulted in the increased reflection loss due to better impedance matching with the free space. The maximum reflection loss of −25.38 dB was achieved for composite containing 85% of BaFe12O19 and 15% of CQD@BaTiO3, for a pellet of thickness 2.75 mm. Also, the maximum −10dB bandwidth (90% absorption) of the composite reaches to 2.7 GHz.

ce:section.ce:para: The advancement of wireless technology for different applications such as mobile operations, satellite communication, and military RADAR has given rise to the problems of electromagnetic (EM) pollution and electromagnetic interference (EMI) [1,2]. To stay in accordance with the recent progress of the electronic and communication sector, the need for developing efficient EM wave or microwave absorber cannot be ignored. An ideal microwave absorbing material (MAM) must provide the specific impedance and signal attenuation to the incident EM wave [3]. Microwave absorbing materials find numerous applications both in the commercial and defense sector. In commercial applications, these materials are used for the reduction of electromagnetic pollution, electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding and anechoic chambers [4,5]. In the defense sector, these materials are used for stealth applications for the prevention of target detection from RADAR [6]. The microwave absorption is achieved by impedance matching of free space with a material-surface interface. The impedance matching ensures maximum power transfer or minimum reflection from the surface [7]. Further, by proper selection of microwave absorbing material, the energy of incident electromagnetic waves can be reduced within the material by various loss mechanisms [3]. So far, various magnetic and dielectric materials have been synthesized to impart magnetic and dielectric losses [8]. Magnetic materials namely, barium hexaferrite [9,10], strontium hexaferrite [11], cobalt ferrite [12], nickel ferrite [13] and zinc ferrite [14] are widely used to provide magnetic loss. But magnetic material alone cannot fulfill the requirements of wide frequency range and high microwave absorption. Therefore, composites of magnetic ferrites with dielectric fillers such as titanium dioxide, barium titanate, calcium titanate, etc. have been explored [15–17]. Also, the microwave absorber materials based on ferrites experiences the problem of high density and narrow bandwidth [18]. Therefore, low-density carbon-based materials like carbon black, carbon nanotubes, reduced graphene oxide, etc. have also been extensively studied in the literature for microwave absorption [19–21].

Barium titanate (BaTiO3) is a ferroelectric ceramic having high value of electrical permittivity and high dielectric constant. The high dielectric constant and high electrical conductivity provides large dielectric losses and thereby the enhance the microwave absorption property [22]. Other application areas of BaTiO3 includes multilayer capacitors, microphones, piezoelectric transducers, and various optoelectronic devices [23]. It is well reported that by changing geometry to fibrous form, several properties such as optical behavior, magnetic behavior, thermal and electrical conductivity can be improved [24,25]. Also, the high specific surface area and structural anisotropy of 1D materials such as nanofibers, nanotubes etc, improves their microwave absorption ability [26,27]. The improved microwave absorption properties in 1-D materials arise due to interfacial polarization effect and confined electron movements [28]. In the present work, the 1-D BaTiO3 fibers are synthesized with the help of the electrospinning technique. Among other reported techniques, electrospinning has been considered the most efficient and simple technique for the fabrication of fibers [25,29]. Several studies have reported that use of carbon quantum dots with dielectric materials like TiO2 could result in improved charge transfer due to reduction in electron-hole recombination [30]. As a result of improved conductivity, the conduction losses can be enhanced for microwave absorption [31]. Therefore in this work, the BaTiO3 fibers are coated with water-soluble carbon quantum dots. The carbon quantum dots are synthesized by a green route using lemon peel.

Barium hexaferrite is a hard ferrite having a high value of saturation magnetization, uni-axial magneto-crystalline anisotropy, and wideband resonance frequency [24]. These properties make barium hexaferrite a widely used magnetic material for applications like magnetic tapes, permanent magnets, speaker magnets, magnetic id cards etc [32]. The resonance frequency of barium hexaferrite is generally above 30 GHz. However, the resonance can be tuned to lower frequencies by different substitutions [10]. Several methods such as sol-gel [33], solid-state reaction [34], microemulsion [35], co-precipitation method [36], etc have been adopted in literature for the synthesis of hexaferrite nanoparticles. The present work involves the synthesis of barium hexaferrite nanoparticles by auto-combustion method. This method is preferred over other reported methods as it produces low-density particles with uniform size distribution, high purity and high crystallinity [37].

Further, the composites of CQD@BaTiO3 fibers and BaFe12O19 nanoparticles are developed by mechanical alloying method using ball-mill followed by heat treatment. The developed composites were tested for microwave absorption properties in the X-band region.

Chemicals used for synthesis of carbon quantum dots are Sodium Hypochlorite (NaOCl, Merck), Sulphuric Acid (H2SO4, ≥98%), and Dichloromethane (CH₂Cl₂, Merck, ≥ 99.9%). Chemicals used for synthesis process of BaTiO3 fibers are barium acetate (Ba(CH3COO)2, Merck, ≥99%), Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Alfa Aesar, M.W. 1,300,000), acetic acid (CH3COOH, Merck, 100%), ethanol (C2H5OH, Merck) and titanium tetraisopropoxide (TTIP, Sigma Aldrich, ≥97%). Chemicals used for barium hexaferrite synthesis are Iron(III) Nitratenonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, Merk, ≥98%), Barium Carbonate (BaCO3, Merck, ≥99%), acetic acid (CH3COOH, Merck, 100%), citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O, Merck, ≥99.5%), and ammonia solution (NH3, Merck, 25%). All chemicals were AR/GR grade products and were used without any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots were synthesized via the green synthesis route using lemon peel as precursor material. The lemon peels were collected from household agricultural waste. In the beginning, 5 g of lemon peels were washed several times with D.I. water and then kept for drying overnight at 100 °C in oven. The dried lemon peels were crushed into small pieces using pestle mortar. To remove the undesired organic impurities, the precursor was treated with 0.1 M H2SO4 for 5 min. The obtained material was washed with D.I. water several times, filtered, and then dried at 100 °C in oven. After completely drying, the sample was dispersed into a 150 mL NaOCl solution for 4 h. The sample was filtrated followed by washing with D.I. water until the pH reaches 7. For the carbonization of the banana peel sample, the obtained sample was kept into Teflon autoclave for 12 h at 200 °C. The carbonized sample was washed with dichloromethane to remove unreacted organic impurities. Finally, the carbon quantum dots were obtained from the solution by centrifugation and filtration followed by drying at 100 °C.
Barium titanate fibers were synthesized by using the conventional sol-gel method followed by the electrospinning technique as reported elsewhere [38].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of core-shell heterostructure of CQD@ BaTiO3 fibers
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of CQD@BaTiO3 core shell, the solution of CQD (molarity 1 mg/mL) was prepared in D.I. water (solution I). 50 mg of BaTiO3 fibers were dissolved in 10 mL of solution I and stirred gently for 6 h at room temperature to form solution II. CQD coated fibers were then collected from solution II by the centrifugation process. The collected fibers were dried at 60 °C. The synthesis route is represented in Fig. 1 .

Barium hexaferrite nanoparticles synthesis was carried out using a sol-gel auto-combustion route. The precursor materials used for reactions were iron nitrate and barium carbonate. The stoichiometric amount of precursor materials were calculated according to the following reaction
BaCO3 + 12Fe(NO3)3+ C6H8O7⇨BaFe12O19 + CO2 + NO2+ H2

Solution (I) was prepared by dissolving the stoichiometric amount of iron nitrate in D.I. water whereas Solution (II) was prepared by dissolving barium carbonate in acetic acid. The above solutions (I) and (II) were mixed and added with citric acid in a 1:1 ratio of metal ions. The pH of the obtained solution was adjusted to 7 by adding an ammonia solution. The solution was subjected to constant heat of 80 °C on a magnetic stirrer until the formation of a wet gel. After further heating to 80 °C the dry gel is formed which undergoes self-ignition to form low density, highly porous barium hexaferrite powder. The obtained powder was crushed using a ball-mill (P7 Premium Line) to obtain the barium hexaferrite nanoparticles. The ball to powder ratio was maintained to 5:1 and powder was crushed for 20 min at 200 rpm. The synthesis route has been depicted in Fig. 2 .

Composites were developed by mechanical alloying of BaFe12O19 nanoparticles and CQD@BaTiO3 fibers and in different weight percentages. Four composites namely BCBT(0), BCBT(5), BCBT(10) and BCBT(15) have been developed. The compositional details of developed composites are shown in Table 1 below.

The samples in the above-mentioned ratios are crushed using ball-mill for 20 min, using the ball to powder weight ratio of 5:1. The composites were heated at 200 °C for 30 min to obtain the final composites.

Fig. 3 shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 3(a)) and TEM micrographs (Fig. 3(b)) of carbon quantum dots. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CQDs synthesized via the green route was obtained through Varian Cary 100, UV Visible spectrophotometer. The spectrum shows an abrupt increase in absorption values from 500 nm to 200 nm. A broad absorption peak is observed at 270 nm which signifies n-π∗ and π-π∗ transitions of –CO and CC bonds [39,40]. The images of the CQD solution under UV light and white light are shown in the inset of Fig. 3(a). The aqueous solution of carbon quantum dots in the water are highly soluble as observed under white light and exhibits bright blue color luminescence under the UV irradiations (inset of Fig. 3). The TEM micrograph of synthesized carbon quantum dots are obtained from Tecnai G2 20 Twin, Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) at 200 kV. The figure obtained, confirms the formation of quantum dots having spherical shaped morphologies. The size of quantum dots is calculated using ImageJ software, as shown in inset of Fig. 3(b). The histogram depicts that 80% of the synthesized carbon quantum dots have a particle size in the range of 2–3 nm.

The developed BaTiO3 fibers, CQD@BaTiO3 fibers, BaFe12O19 nanoparticles and their composite BCBT(15) have been analyzed using SEM and EDAX analytical techniques. The fibers in Fig. 4 (a) and Fig. 4(b) are analyzed through Tescan MIRA 3, Field Emission Secondary Electron Microscope. The synthesis of BaTiO3 fibers can be confirmed from Fig. 4(a). The figure shows the uniform and continuous fibers of an average diameter of 535 nm, calculated using ImageJ software. The CQD@BaTiO3 fibers are shown in Fig. 4(b). As evident from the figure, the CQD@ BaTiO3 fibers have increased diameter. The average diameter of CQD@BaTiO3 fibers is calculated to be 683 nm. From the figure, it can be observed that the addition of CQD to the fibers has not introduced any secondary phase in the FESEM results. This indicates that the coating of carbon quantum dots on the surface of fibers resulting in a core-shell structure. This finding can also be justified based on the increased diameter of fibers.

The barium hexaferrite nanoparticles in Fig. 4(c) are analyzed via Carl Zeiss MA15, Secondary Electron Microscope. From the obtained figure it can be observed that hexagonal plate-like shaped barium hexaferrite particles have been developed in “as-synthesized” conditions and there is no need for further heat-treatment process. The hexagonal plate-like structure of barium hexaferrite tends to have increased interfacial polarization because of the high surface area. The increased interfacial polarization results in high power dissipation for applications in microwave absorption medium [20,41].

In present work three composites, as mentioned in Table 1, have been synthesized. The formation of BaFe12O19/CQD@BaTiO3 composite has been confirmed using the SEM micrograph of BCBT(15) composite as shown in Fig. 4(d). The SEM micrograph shows the presence of BaTiO3 fiber along with relatively smaller particles of hexagonal shaped morphology. These agglomerated hexagonal shaped particles represent the BaFe12O19 nanoparticles. Furthermore, the EDAX pattern of the developed BCBT(15) composite has been obtained in Fig. 4(e) to determine the elements present in the composite. The EDAX pattern shows the existence of C, O, Ti, Fe and Ba elements in the material which is due to the presence of CQD coated BaTiO3 fibers along with BaFe12O19 particles.

Fig. 5 (a) shows the indexed XRD pattern of BaTiO3 fibers and CQD@BaTiO3 fibers obtained via Panalytical X’Pert Pro Diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation. The high-intensity perovskite peaks of BaTiO3 fibers annealed at 750 °C is evident from the indexed XRD patterns. The 100% peak of perovskite BaTiO3 is observed at 2θ = 32°. The reduction in peak intensity is observed for CQD@BaTiO3 fibers. This observation could be due to the increase in the amorphous character of CQD@BaTiO3 fibers as compared to highly crystalline BaTiO3 fibers. The XRD pattern of CQD@BaTiO3 fibers doesn’t signify a major change in peak positions except an induced hump of carbon between 2θ = 25° to 2θ = 30°. This induced hump confirms the presence of carbon with BaTiO3. The indexed XRD pattern of BaFe12O19 nanoparticles is obtained from the Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer and is shown in Fig. 5(b). The figure confirms that BaFe12O19 particles are synthesized in “as synthesis” condition (JCDS No 01-072-0738) and the post heat-treatment process is not needed. The 100% peak of (114) pale is observed at 2θ = 35.73°. The average crystallite size of developed BaFe12O19 nanoparticles is derived from the Debye-Scherrer equation and is found to be about 15.08 nm.

Fig. 5(c) shows the XRD pattern of the developed BCBT(15) composite. The XRD pattern resembles the peaks corresponding to BaFe12O19 and BaTiO3 phases without the presence of any other secondary phase. The 100% peak of BaTiO3 fiber corresponding to (101) plane is observed at 31.54° while the 100% peak of BaFe12O19 particle is observed at 35.63°. A small hump is observed near 2θ = 15° which confirms the presence of CQD.

PerkinElmer Spectrum two Spectrometer is used for the FT-IR analysis of developed materials. The FTIR spectrum of BaTiO3 and CQD@BaTiO3 fibers are shown in Fig. 6 (a). The absorption peak at position 3438 cm-1 is due to –OH bond stretching vibrations while the peak at 584 cm-1 is due to Ti–O bond stretching vibrations. The peak of 1383 cm-1 might be due to the stretch vibration of CO3-2 of residual barium carbonate (BaCO3) [42]. The peaks at 1030 cm-1 and 1636 cm-1 correspond to stretching vibrations of C–O and CO bonds respectively. The small peaks at 2925 cm-1 and 2850 cm-1 corresponds to vibrations due to –OH bond stretch. From the figure, it can be observed that, FTIR spectrum of CQD@BaTiO3 fibers remained almost similar to that obtained for BaTiO3 fibers, except an additional peak arising at 1589 cm-1 due to aromatic C–C stretch vibrations. This additional peak is due to the addition of carbon quantum dots. Also, the peak due to –OH stretch vibrations decreases while the peak corresponding to vibrations of Ti–O bond stretch increases for CQD@BaTiO3 fibers. The FTIR spectrum of developed barium hexaferrite nanoparticles is shown in Fig. 6(b). The peaks at 433 cm-1, 542 cm-1, and 588 cm-1 correspond to Fe–O vibrations of tetrahedral and octahedral sites respectively [43]. The peaks at 1475 cm-1 and 1560 cm-1 signifies vibrations due to N–O stretch while the peak at 1640 cm-1 signifies stretch vibrations of N–H bond [44]. The peaks at 3743 cm-1 and 3448 cm-1 are due to the vibration of the O–H bond stretch. The small peaks at 2928 cm-1 and 2944 cm-1 might be due to the vibration of dimer –OH stretch.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Complex permittivity and complex permeability study
ce:section.ce:para: The dielectric and magnetic properties of a microwave absorber are determined by complex permittivity (ε = ε’ - jε”) and complex permeability (μ = μ’ - jμ”) of the material. The real part of permittivity and permeability signifies storage whereas the imaginary part signifies losses [28]. Greater the losses better would be the microwave absorption efficiency. The complex permittivity and complex permeability study of developed composites is carried out using Network Analyzer (Agilent E8364B PNA series). The complex permittivity of developed composites as a function of frequency is shown in Fig. 7 . The real part of electric permittivity shows an increasing trend with an increase in content of CQD@BaTiO3 fibers in the composite (Fig. 7(a)). The average value of real permittivity increased from 5.57 for BCBT(0) to 9.15 for BCBT(15). A similar trend was observed for the imaginary part of complex permittivity (Fig. 7(b)). The figure shows various resonance peaks in X-band for imaginary permittivity. A sharp resonance peak is observed for developed composites between 10 GHz and 11 GHz. The average value of imaginary permittivity increased from 0.67 for BCBT(0) to 1.33 for BCBT(15). The possible reason for an increase in the complex permittivity of the composites might be the high dielectric value of BaTiO3 material.

The magnetic permeability of developed composites as a function of frequency is shown in Fig. 8 . The real permeability shows the decreasing trend with increasing content of CQD@BaTiO3 fibers in the composite (Fig. 8(a)). The average value of real permeability decreased from 1.046 for BCBT(0) to 1.023 for BCBT(15). The decreasing pattern in the real part might be due to a decrease in the magnetic behavior of the composite with a reduction of barium hexaferrite. The average value of imaginary part first increased from 0.079 for BCBT(0) to 1.05 for BCBT(10) and then marginally decreased to 0.101 for BCBT(15) (Fig. 8(b)). This trend in the real and imaginary part of complex permeability signifies that magnetic losses of the composites have been increased with increasing content of CQD@BaTiO3 fibers. The increased permittivity values might be due to the fact that the CQD@BaTiO3 fibers act as a core-shell heterostructure, where the introduction of the potential barrier at the interface causes an increase in the charge carrier concentration and the mobility [45]. As a result, the conduction is increased which causes an increase in permittivity values. With the further increase of CQD@BaTiO3 content in the composite, the available functions groups are increased resulting in easy dipole alignment [46] and hence the permittivity is increased from BCBT(5) to BCBT(15).

To account for electromagnetic energy losses within the medium, the tangent loss has been calculated from permittivity and permeability values using the equation:
(1)
tan
δ e
=
ε "
ε ′
(2)
tan
δ m
=
μ "
μ ′
where, tan δe represents dielectric loss tangent and tan δm represents magnetic loss tangent. Higher the value of tangent loss, the higher will be the loss of electromagnetic energy within the medium and better would be the performance of microwave absorber [47]. The plot of tan δe versus frequency is shown in Fig. 9 (a). The figure shows multiple resonance peaks in X-band with a major peak emerging between 10 and 11 GHz. This pattern is in accordance with permittivity values obtained for the composites. From the figure it can be observed that by increasing the content of CQD@BaTiO3 fiber, the dielectric tangent loss shifts towards higher value. The average dielectric loss tangent values observed for BCBT(0), BCBT(5), BCBT(10) and BCBT(15) are 0.121, 0.138, 0.142 and 0.146 respectively. The plot of tan δm as a function of frequency is shown in Fig. 9(b). The plot shows various resonance peaks which are in accordance with permeability curves obtained in Fig. 8. The average value of tangent loss first increases from BCBT(0) to BCBT(10) and then decreases slightly to BCBT(15). The average values of magnetic loss tangent obtained for BCBT(0), BCBT(5), BCBT(10) and BCBT(15) are 0.075, 0.097, 0.102 and 0.099 respectively.
To determine the microwave absorption efficiency of developed composites, the reflection loss studies have been carried out in X-band.

The reflection loss (RL) is the ratio of reflected power to the incident power given by Ref. [48]:
(3)
R L = 20
log
10
|
Z
i n
−
Z o
Z
i n
+
Z o
|
where Zo is the intrinsic impedance of the free space and Zin is the input impedance of material/surface interface given by:
(4)
Z
i n
=
Z o
μ r
ε r
tanh
(
j
(
2 π f d
c
)
μ r
ε r
)
where, εr is the relative permittivity, μr is relative permeability, f is the frequency of operation, d is the coating thickness and c is the velocity of light.

The higher value of RL signifies higher attenuation of EM wave within the medium and reduced reflections from the surface. From equation (5), the −10dB reflection loss represents 90% absorption and −20dB value represents 99% absorption. The reflection loss curves as a function of thickness and frequency are shown in Fig. 10 . The relation of reflection loss with thickness can be explained based on the EM phase cancellation effect. When the incident EM wave and the EM wave emergent from the medium are out of phase by 180°, both the wave will cancel out each other [3]. For this to happen, the coating thickness (d) of the material should follow the equation [49]:
(5)
d =
n λ
4
where, n is the cardinal number (n = 1.3.5 …. .)

λ is the wavelength of incident EM wave given by
(6)
λ =
c
f
|
ε r
|
|
μ r
|

From equations (5) and (6)
(7)
d =
n c
4 f
|
ε r
|
|
μ r
|

From Fig. 10, it can be observed that, by adding CQD@BaTiO3 fibers in the composite, the reflection loss is shifted towards the region of lower coating thickness. This observation can be justified from equation (7). Since the permittivity of the material has increased by the addition of CQD@BaTiO3 fibers, the reflection loss peak has shifted towards the value of lower coating thickness. Also, it is very clear from the figure that the reflection loss peak has shifted towards a lower frequency region. The shifting of reflection loss peak towards lower frequency might be due to the reduction in magneto-crystalline anisotropy of barium hexaferrite with addition of CQD@barium titanate fibers [50]. The maximum reflection loss is obtained for BCBT(15) composite for the coating thickness of 2.75 mm. The high magnetic and dielectric losses of the developed composite could have been the possible reason for improved microwave absorbing results of developed composites [51,52].

The obtained high reflection loss results of the developed composites can be explained on the basis of impedance matching. The condition of minimum reflection from the surface can be achieved by impedance matching. For this, the intrinsic impedance of the free space should be equal to the input impedance of the material/surface interface [3]. This condition is represented as [53]:
(8)
N o r m a l i z e d I m p e d a n c e =
Z
i n
Z o
= 1

The plot of reflection loss and normalized impedance at coating thickness of 2.75 mm is shown in Fig. 11 . From the figure, it can be observed that better performance of BCBT(15) composite at thickness 2.75 mm is due to the better impedance matching of BCBT(15) composite with impedance of free space.

The maximum reflection loss of −25.38 is achieved for BCBT(15) composite at 10.21 GHz. The obtained reflection loss (RL) values and −10dB bandwidth and maximum reflection loss of developed composites are shown in Table 2 .

The composites based on CQD@BaTiO3 fibers and BaFe12O19 nanoparticles have been successfully synthesized. The developed composites are studied for microwave absorption in X-band (8–12 GHz) microwave region. The addition of CQD@BaTiO3 fibers in the composite medium resulted in improved dielectric and magnetic properties of the medium. The improvement in properties causes better impedance matching and increased reflection loss of the developed composites. The maximum value of reflection loss obtained is −25.38 dB for BCBT(15) composite for coating thickness 2.75 mm. Also, the −10dB bandwidth (90% absorption) of BCBT(15) composite reaches to 2.7 GHz i.e. 65% of X-band.

Shivanshu Goel: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Ankit Tyagi: Methodology, Validation, Visualization. Avesh Garg: Formal analysis, Data curation. Sumit Kumar: Investigation. Himangshu Bhusan Baskey: Data curation. Raju Kumar Gupta: Writing - review & editing, Resources, Supervision. Sachin Tyagi: Writing - review & editing, Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to Director, Directorate of Extramural Research & Intellectual Property Rights, DRDO Delhi for providing financial support to carry out research. The authors would also like to acknowledge the Director, DRDO-DMSRDE, Kanpur for providing support in carrying out the reflection loss study. The authors are thankful to the Director, CSIR-CSIO, and IIT- Kanpur for the support and the encouragement provided for the present work.
